coredata.dc:title: Optical and electronic properties of TiO2/GOQDs composites: A combined experimental and first-principles calculations study
coredata.dc:creator: Jauja-Ccana, V.R.
Córdova Huamán, Allison V.
Feliciano, Gustavo T.
La Rosa-Toro Gómez, Adolfo
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the optical and electronic properties of a TiO2/graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) composite were studied by experimental and computational methods. The experimental procedures were made on TiO2 and GOQDs nanoparticles synthesized by a sol-gel method and one-pot electrochemical carbonization, respectively. The characterization of these materials and the TiO2/GOQDs composite was made using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron microscopy (XPS), FT-IR spectroscopy, and dynamic light scattering (DLS). The experimental measurements of the optical properties of these materials by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy indicate that the addition of GOQDs promotes the expansion of the radiation absorption of TiO2 towards the visible range. Similarly, photoelectrochemical tests performed to determine the electronic properties show that GOQDs improve the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, increasing the photocatalytic activity of the TiO2. These results were further contrasted with a computational study based on first-principles DFT-D3 calculations, using the projector augmented wave (PAW) method on TiO2, pristine GQDs, GOQDs, TiO2/GQDs, and TiO2/GOQDs clusters. The computational results showed that GOQDs exhibit properties of a p-type semiconductor due to the presence of oxygenated groups. Therefore, when used as TiO2/GOQDs composite, these form a type II heterojunction, increasing the photocatalytic activity of the material.

head.ce:title: Optical and electronic properties of TiO2/GOQDs composites: A combined experimental and first-principles calculations study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the optical and electronic properties of a TiO2/graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) composite were studied by experimental and computational methods. The experimental procedures were made on TiO2 and GOQDs nanoparticles synthesized by a sol-gel method and one-pot electrochemical carbonization, respectively. The characterization of these materials and the TiO2/GOQDs composite was made using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron microscopy (XPS), FT-IR spectroscopy, and dynamic light scattering (DLS). The experimental measurements of the optical properties of these materials by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy indicate that the addition of GOQDs promotes the expansion of the radiation absorption of TiO2 towards the visible range. Similarly, photoelectrochemical tests performed to determine the electronic properties show that GOQDs improve the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, increasing the photocatalytic activity of the TiO2. These results were further contrasted with a computational study based on first-principles DFT-D3 calculations, using the projector augmented wave (PAW) method on TiO2, pristine GQDs, GOQDs, TiO2/GQDs, and TiO2/GOQDs clusters. The computational results showed that GOQDs exhibit properties of a p-type semiconductor due to the presence of oxygenated groups. Therefore, when used as TiO2/GOQDs composite, these form a type II heterojunction, increasing the photocatalytic activity of the material.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have aroused great attraction due to their interesting optical and electronic properties produced by their metal-free semiconductor behavior [1–3]. Because of the quantum confinement and the edge effects [4], these properties depend on their size [5], shape [4,6], state of aggregation [7], doping with heteroatoms (B, N, S, Se) [8–10], and functionalization [3]. Previous studies report that the selected method for the synthesis of these materials, top-down or bottom-up methods [11], determines their oxidation degree [12]. For instance, the electrochemical carbonization of alcohol, one of the most scalable-up method to produce GQDs [13–15], uses oxidant agents during the procedure. In this process, oxygenated groups (–OH and –COOH) are formed on the edge of GQDs, producing oxygenated derivatives of GQDs known as graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) [12,16,17].

Despite their differences, GQDs and GOQDs have been used without distinction for many applications, like bioimage, optical and electrochemical sensors, diodes, photo/electrocatalysis, and others [13–15]. In photocatalytic processes, the addition of GQDs to metallic oxide semiconductors has proved to increase the photocatalytic efficiency of the material [18–22]. Photocatalytic experiments with TiO2/GQDs composites have shown that GQDs play a crucial role in the photogenerated electron-hole charge separation (recombination) and increase the absorption range towards the visible light [23–28]. However, theoretical studies of the effect of GQDs on the optical and electronic behavior of TiO2 composites have not been fully addressed. Furthermore, there are no previous studies on the influence of the oxygenated groups (GOQDs) on these results.

In this work, we studied the optical and electronic properties of TiO2, GOQDs, and TiO2/GOQDs composites through experimental and computational methods. UV–Vis spectroscopy and photoelectrochemical tests were used to study these properties experimentally. The effect of the addition of oxygenated groups (–OH and –COOH) on the optical and electronic properties of GQDs (formation of GOQDs) was evaluated by first-principle calculation techniques, through the analysis of the projected density of states (PDoS). These techniques were further used to analyze the effect of GQDs and GOQDs inclusion on the electronic, optical, and charge transport properties of TiO2 composites. Our experimental and theoretical results provide a comprehensive explanation of the GOQDs absorption expansion towards the visible light region and the improvement of the charge separation in photocatalytic processes.

GOQDs were synthesized by an electrochemical carbonization method [13–15] using ethanol as a solvent and carbon source. The electrochemical cell consisted of KOH 0.01 M and ethanol as an electrolyte and two graphite rods as anode and cathode. A constant voltage (10 V) was applied to the system for 4 h until the initially transparent solution turned brown. The high potential applied to the system oxidizes the ethanol, forming –OH radicals and, consequently, GOQDs [29].

TiO2 was synthesized by a sol–gel method. Briefly, titanium isopropoxide was added dropwise to a dilute solution of HNO3 with vigorous stirring for 6 h at 65 °C, then washed with water and calcined at 200 °C for 3 h. The TiO2 nanoparticles obtained were dispersed in 20 ml of the ethanolic suspension of GOQDs using an ultrasonic bath for 30 min at 60 °C to form the TiO2/GOQDs composite. The resultant solid was washed twice with deionized water and dried for 24 h at 60 °C, obtaining a pale yellow colored TiO2/GOQDs composite. The TiO2 and TiO2/GOQDs samples were finally calcined at 200 °C for 2 h. For the photoelectrochemical tests, TiO2 and TiO2/GOQDs solids were dip-coated on FTO glass substrates and dried at 100 °C for 30 min.

The samples were prepared in KBr pellets for their characterization with a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) technique using a Perkin Elmer GX spectrophotometer and a scan from 4000 cm to 400 cm with a 1 cm resolution. The size distribution of TiO2 and TiO2/GOQDs was determined by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) analysis, using a particle size analyzer (Brookhaven 90 Plus). The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using a Rigaku D/Max 2550 X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Ka radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) in the range of 10 to 80° (2θ) at room temperature. The UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy measurements were taken at room temperature with a UV-3150 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on an ESCALAB 250 Xi (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA), using non-monochromatized Mg–K X-ray as the excitation source. The binding energies for the samples were calibrated by setting the measured binding energy of C 1s to 284.60 eV. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and electrochemical and photoelectrochemical measurements of the synthesized compound were made using a potentiostat/galvanostat (Autolab PGSTAT302, MetrOhm, UK with FRA). The EIS measurements were recorded over a frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. The applied bias voltage was set to the open-circuit voltage in a [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− 5 mM and KCl 0.1 M electrolyte.

All the computational studies were performed using the most used structures in the literature. The most stable structure of (TiO2)n nanoclusters is reached with n = 6, which has the highest formation probability and the lowest nucleation energy [30–34]. GQDs are polyaromatic particles that can be represented by the minimum unit of these, the coronene. Similar units have been widely used as a calculation model in different applications, such as hydrogenation, determination of magnetic properties, and photovoltaic processes [35–37]. Oxygenated groups as –COOH and –OH were included in pristine GQDs to simulate the characteristic structure of a material treated with oxidizing agents (GOQDs) [38]. The oxygenated groups were placed at the edge of the molecule considering the high reactivity of these positions and the high stability of the basal plane, similar to the graphene structure [39,40].

First principle calculations of the density functional theory (DFT) were performed using the Quantum Espresso package [41]. These calculations were carried out using the projector augmented wave potentials (PAW) [42] with the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) [43] of the Perdew-Burke-Eznzerhof (PBE) [44] method. The wave functions were expanded in the plane-wave basis set with a kinetic energy cutoff of 550 eV. The Brillouin zone was sampled at the Γ point. All the structures were fully optimized using the convergence criteria of 10−6 eV for the energy and 10−3 eV/Å for the forces on each atom. Also, a 15.0 Å vacuum region was added in all directions to effectively eliminate the possible interactions between the structure and its periodic images. These GGA-PBE functionals have been used by several works to study oxide clusters within TiO2 [45–47] and describe the electronic properties of carbonaceous materials [48–50]. In addition to these functionals, Grimme's semiempirical DFT-D3 scheme of van der Waals dispersion correction was adopted to better describe the experimentally observed TiO2-GOQDs interaction [24,51].

The optical absorption spectra were computed by TD-DFT using the Liouville-Lanczos approach [52,53]. Fig. 1 A–C presents the optimized geometries of the studied system, which will be used to determine the optical and electronic properties of TiO2, GQDs/TiO2, and TiO2/GOQDs. These structures consider the van der Waals interactions observed in these materials [51]. The displayed images show that GOQDs do not modify the TiO2 structure, as the experimental results also suggest.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the materials (Fig. 2 ) demonstrate that GOQDs are attached to the TiO2 nanoparticles [19,27]. The wide absorption band around 3410 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl –OH and methyl –CH groups of GOQDs [27]. The asymmetric stretching of CO in the –COOH group is represented by the peak at 1650 cm−1 [24]. The aromatic CC and the C-OH vibrations [54] are seen in the peaks at 1633 cm−1 and 1375 cm−1, respectively. Also, the symmetric and anti-symmetric O–C–O stretching is represented by the peaks at 1575 and 1416 cm−1 [27]. The bands at 832, 678, and 649 cm−1 are assigned to the aromatic C–H bend. Finally, the intense band observed between 1100 and 700 cm−1 can be attributed to the Ti-O-Ti stretching vibrations [27].

Fig. S1 shows a FE-SEM of the composite where the homogeneous distribution of the GOQDs is observed, similar to the material obtained in other references [55]. This also coincides with the DLS results, which indicate that there is no significant difference between the size of the TiO2 and the composite (Fig. S2). This suggests that the size of the GOQDs (<5 nm) has a depreciable effect on the size of the composite.

The X-Ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of TiO2 and TiO2/GOQDs composite show that the addition of GOQDs does not affect the crystalline structure of TiO2 (Fig. 3 ). As can be seen, there are no differences in the location and intensity of the peaks obtained from GOQDs/TiO2 composite and TiO2, indicating that the composite conserves the crystallinity of the metallic oxide [19,22,39]. In contrast, other studies have shown that the TiO2 composites obtained with graphite flakes do show a significant variation in their diffractograms [56]. The peaks displayed in the figure (25.3°, 30.6°, 37.7°, 48.1°, 54.4°, 63.1°, 69.4°, and 74.9°) indicate the presence of anatase as a predominant phase and also rutile. The low definition of these peaks is due to the low degree of crystallinity of the samples caused by their thermal treatment at 200 °C. The crystal size of these samples was calculated using The Scherrer equation from the XRD patterns of TiO2 (3.94 nm) and TiO2/GOQDs (4.13 nm).

The XPS spectrum was obtained to study the chemical composition of the TiO2/GOQDs surface (Fig. 4 A–D). Fig. 4D shows the deconvoluted spectrum of the C1s peak at 283.1 and 284.8 eV, which represent the CC bond [57], and the CC energy binding. Four additional peaks at 285.4, 286.5, 288.3, and 289.1 eV represent COH [58], CO, CO, and O-CO bonding. The high-resolution spectrum of the O1s peak (Fig. 4C) is fitted to three gaussian peaks at 529.9, 531.4, and 532.6 eV. The first one indicates the crystal lattice oxygen Ti-O-Ti [19], and the last two peaks are assigned to OC and OC, respectively [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the optical and electronic properties of GQDs and GOQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of GOQDs were studied by UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 5 ). The experimental spectrum shows two characteristic absorption bands between 230 and 240 nm and 340–360 nm, attributed to the π → π* transition of the CC bond and the n → π* transition of the CO bond [60–64]. These bands are associated with the oxidation degree and the size of the material [60,61,65]. The calculated electronic absorption spectra of GQDs and GOQDs (PBE functional) present some differences due to the n → π* electronic transition of the oxygenated functional groups CO of GOQDs [62–64]. Although there is a small displacement between the experimental and calculated bands, these measurements are in good agreement.

Fig. S4 displays the hybridization of the frontier orbitals, which shows that the isosurface of the HOMO and LUMO of GOQDs present an electronic delocalization of the carbons that form a sizeable π-conjugated system. As can be seen, the oxygenated groups in GOQDs do not significantly affect the distribution of the HOMO and LUMO frontier orbitals of the GQDs sheets. However, the lower occupied levels, HOMO-1 and HOMO-2, of GQDs and GOQDs present a variation. In the HOMOs distribution of the GOQDs, we can see that this is concentrated mainly around the oxygenated groups (–COOH).

The energies of the border orbitals of GQDs and GOQDs were obtained using the projected density of states (PDoS) Fig. S5 indicates the difference between the HOMO (EGQDs = −4.90 and EGOQDs = −5.07 eV) and LUMO (EGQDs = −2.04 and E GOQDs = −2.64 eV) energies of GQDs and GOQDs. This figure also displays the band gap reduction of GOQDs (2.43 eV) compared with GQDs (2.86 eV), which is in good agreement with other computational and experimental results [64,66,67]. The addition of oxygen 2p orbitals in CO (COOH) and OH functional groups introduces electronic states into the band gap of GQDs [68]. These groups, especially COOH, present an electron-withdrawing effect that gives a p-type semiconductor behavior to the GOQDs [1,2] compared to the n-type semiconductor nature of GQDs [1,69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the optical and electronic properties of TiO2/GOQDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental results have shown that the attachment of GOQDs to TiO2 changes the optical properties of the latter, increasing the radiation absorption of TiO2 towards the visible light region. Fig. 6 displays the experimental and calculated UV–Vis spectra of TiO2 and TiO2/GOQDs. As observed, TiO2 absorbs radiation in the UV region, around 380 nm. In contrast, the absorption spectrum of the TiO2/GOQDs composite expands towards the visible region. The calculated absorption spectra of TiO2, TiO2/GQDs, and TiO2/GOQDs coincide with these results. In these calculations, the inclusion of oxygenated groups in the TiO2/GOQDs composite causes a red-shifting of the absorption spectrum relative to TiO2 and TiO2/GQDs.

To study the effect of GQDs and GOQDs on the electronic properties of TiO2 composites, the electronic density of states (DoS) and PDoS of these compounds were examined (Figs. 7 and 8
, respectively). PDoS of TiO2 suggests that the valence and conduction band are determined by the oxygen 2p orbital and the titanium 3d orbital, respectively. The underestimation of the band gap (Eg = 2.52 eV) in these measurements is typical in these calculations [30,51] and can be attributed to the mid-gap state associated with the low coordination state of the surface atoms [70].

The addition of GQDs and GOQDs in the composites induces sharp occupied molecular energy levels into the band gap of the semiconductor. The composite of TiO2 and GOQDs form a type II semiconductor heterojunction, which presents the most efficient charge separation, as has been experimentally observed in different studies [28,54,71–73]. HOMOs of GOQDs and GQDs are not coupled at the valence band, but their LUMOs are coupled at the conduction band of TiO2, facilitating the electron transfer between materials [74]. In particular, the electronic states produced by the oxygenated species of GOQDs, mainly carbonyl (CO) in COOH groups, are near the TiO2 valence band, and therefore, contribute to the charge transfer.

Figs. 7 and S6 show the electron density of frontier orbitals of TiO2, TiO2/GQDs, and TiO2/GOQDs. In composites, we can see that the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO, HOMO-1, HOMO-2, HOMO-6, and HOMO-8) are distributed over the entire π-conjugated systems, contributing to the radiation absorption, as observed in previous works [51]. The addition of carbonyl groups (HOMO-7 and HOMO-8) of GOQDs in the TiO2 composite introduces new electronic states that cause a red-shifting of the absorption, compared to TiO2/GQDs composite, promoting a better charge separation [75].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy is an efficient method to study interfacial properties and can be easily corroborated by computational methods because it depends on charge distribution [76]. The diameter of the semicircles in the Nyquist plots (Fig. 9 ) is a measurement of the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) of the materials. Compared with TiO2, the TiO2/GOQDs composite shows a smaller internal resistance, suggesting that this material has a better charge transport [77] and an improved photoactivity. Electrostatic map potentials of TiO2, TiO2/GQDs, and TiO2/GOQDs are displayed in Fig. S7, where red areas represent negative potentials, blue areas the positive potentials, and green and yellow are intermediate zones. The electrostatic map of TiO2 is dominated by red and green areas due to the presence of oxygen and titanium atoms in its structure; these atoms cause the electrostatic repulsion of anionic species, such as [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−, and the increment of the charge transfer resistance [78]. The addition of GOQDs on the semiconductor surface to form the composites equilibrates the negatively charged surface, improving the redox couple [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− mobility towards the surface electrode and increasing the charge transfer rate.

To study the photogenerated charge separation mechanism and electron transfer performance, cyclic voltammetries and photocurrent responses were measured on TiO2 and TiO2/GOQDs coated on FTO. These tests were performed under UV-light illumination (370 nm, 10 W) at a bias voltage of 0.5 V in a Na2SO4 0.1 M aqueous solution. Cyclic voltammograms (Fig. 10 A) show an improvement of the photocatalytic processes (the photocurrent increases between 1.2 and 3 times) at low negative bias when the composite is used. This effect occurs because there is a hydrogen evolution on this composite [19,27,54]. Photocurrent responses (Fig. 10B) show an initial photocurrent in the TiO2/GOQDs sample due to the unsustained generation of an abundant number of electron-hole pairs [54,79]. In this case, GOQDs on the surface of TiO2 serve as electron reservoirs and solid-state electron transfer reagents, which trap photoinduced electrons from the conduction band of TiO2. These results are in good agreement with previous photocatalytic applications of TiO2/carbonaceous materials composites [56].

The mechanism that explains the photoelectrocatalytic results under UV light is illustrated by the energy diagram displayed in Fig. 11 . When the composite is illuminated with UV light, the TiO2 and the GOQDs absorb the photons, forming e− photo-electrons and positive h+ holes. The photo-electrons in the conduction band of TiO2 are transferred to the GOQDs when a cathodic potential is applied to the electrode. In this stage, electrons in the conduction band of GOQDs can reduce water and sulfate ions [80]. Similarly, the holes formed in the TiO2 are transferred to the HOMO of the GOQDs. These holes cannot oxidize the water because of their higher energy levels, which explains why GOQDs act as the catalytic material for reducing H2O [19,25,77]. When a positive potential (vs. Ag/AgCl) is applied, the photogenerated electrons in the TiO2 can be transferred to the electrode or the GOQDs. These photo-electrons generated in the GOQDs can react with water or be transferred to the TiO2 and later to the electrode (instantaneous photocurrent observed in Fig. 10.B). However, due to the higher electron transfer rate of GOQDs towards the solution, the number of electrons sent to the electrode will be lower in the composite than in pure TiO2.

The results obtained in this work help evaluate the importance of the functional groups on GQDs because they have a measurable effect on their electronic properties. These properties also affect the photocatalytic properties of composites based on functionalized GQDs with semiconductors. Future works can study the effect of other functionalizations with electron donors (OH, NH2, F, etc.) or acceptors (COOH, PO4, etc.). Understanding the effect of this functionalization on the optoelectronic properties of semiconductors, like TiO2, can help develop tailored photocatalytic systems (I, II, III, or IV) for applications like water splitting, electrooxidation, or electroreduction.

In this work, the optical and electronic properties of TiO2, GOQDs, and TiO2/GOQDs composite have been studied using experimental and computational methods. The experimental absorbance obtained by UV–Vis spectroscopy shows an increase in the absorption range of TiO2 and GOQDs when they are used as a composite. Similarly, the electrochemical tests present an increase in the generated photocurrent of the TiO2/GOQDs composite during the photoelectrochemical reduction. The calculated measurements of the optical properties of TiO2/GQDs and TiO2/GOQDs composites indicate that GOQDs promote an expansion of the radiation absorption towards visible light, due to the presence of oxygenated species (COOH). Similarly, calculated electronic properties indicate that GQDs and GOQDs adhered to the interface of TiO2 add electronic states to the band gap of this semiconductor, allowing the expansion of the radiation absorption range. On the other hand, TiO2 and GOQDs form a type II semiconductor heterojunction that allows a better separation of the photogenerated hole-electron pairs. The presence of functional groups (CO) in GQDs transforms this material into a p-type semiconductor, introducing their electronic states near the HOMO of TiO2 and facilitating the charge transfer. Energetic diagram results suggest that the TiO2/GOQDs species would be an active and efficient photocatalyst for water splitting, where TiO2 is used for O2 evolution and GOQDs, for H2 evolution.
